ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for chicken pakora; closest defensible match is fried chicken food product (FOODON_03315091). Chickpea-battered deep-fried chicken preparation is not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (chicken meat, coated and fried) is the closest defensible match. Specific pakora preparation with chickpea flour batter is not individually coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken pakora as a specific food concept. SNOMED CT food coding is limited for prepared ethnic dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer serving of chicken pakora is approximately 180 g, equivalent to 4–6 pieces, as commonly served at Indian restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) — chickpea flour (besan) and spice composition; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (FDC ID 171477)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from two approved primary sources: (1) ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for chickpea flour (besan) batter composition and Indian spice profile; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried) for the fried chicken meat component. The composite per-100g values reflect a typical chicken pakora formulation: approximately 60% boneless chicken, 30% chickpea flour batter, and 10% absorbed frying oil. Input values from the GrabFood record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these two approved sources and retained where consistent. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and extended micronutrient data are not reliably available for this composite preparation in either approved source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Chickpea flour (besan) analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken pakora as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being chickpea flour (besan), which has a published GI of approximately 39–45 per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, Table of GI values) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The high protein (19.4g/100g) and fat (16.7g/100g) content of the dish further attenuate postprandial glucose response below the pure besan GI. Glycemic load per 180g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 20.0) / 100 = 9.0, where carbohydrate per 180g serving = 11.1 × 1.8 = 20.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 417 mg per 100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold; a standard 180g serving delivers approximately 750 mg sodium, representing 33–50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken pakora to occasional small portions, reduce salt in the marinade and batter to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g, and avoid high-sodium dipping condiments; air-frying or baking is a lower-sodium, lower-calorie alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Chickpea flour (besan) batter confers a low GI (~45) and moderate carbohydrate load (11.1g/100g; GL ~9 per 180g serving), but the high fat content from deep-frying (16.7g/100g) can contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken pakora can be consumed in moderation by individuals with type 2 diabetes given its low GI and high protein content (19.4g/100g), but portion size should be limited to one serving (180g), high-sugar dipping chutneys should be avoided, and air-frying or baking is preferred to reduce fat-driven insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 16.7g total fat and 4.4g saturated fat per 100g, alongside 417 mg sodium and 61 mg cholesterol; a 180g serving delivers approximately 30g total fat, 8g saturated fat, and 750 mg sodium, all of which are clinically significant for cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat chicken pakora as an occasional food only, limit serving size to no more than 90–100g, choose air-fried or baked preparation to reduce saturated fat and total fat, and avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments to manage combined sodium and lipid burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium at 417 mg/100g can worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken pakora due to AGE formation; air-frying or baking retains the anti-inflammatory benefits of turmeric and ginger-garlic while eliminating the pro-inflammatory frying impact, and salt in the marinade should be reduced to lower sodium below 400 mg/100g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Pakora (also spelled Chicken Pakoda; Hindi: चिकन पकोड़ा; Tamil: சிக்கன் பகோரா) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack consisting of boneless chicken pieces marinated in a blend of Indian spices — typically turmeric, cumin, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, and salt — coated in a thick chickpea flour (besan) batter and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy and tender inside. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely consumed across India, Pakistan, and the South Asian diaspora, including Singapore, where it is commonly served as an appetizer or street food snack. The chickpea flour batter imparts a nutty, slightly earthy flavour and a characteristically crunchy texture. Nutritionally, chicken pakora is protein-dense (approximately 19.4g protein per 100g) and moderate in carbohydrates (11.1g/100g), with the chickpea flour contributing a low glycemic index (~45) and dietary fibre (1.7g/100g). However, deep-frying significantly increases fat content (16.7g/100g, of which 4.4g is saturated fat) and caloric density (~267 kcal/100g). Sodium is elevated at 417 mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger-garlic) provide polyphenol benefits but cannot offset the pro-inflammatory impact of deep-frying for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A standard appetizer serving is approximately 180g (4–6 pieces), delivering roughly 480 kcal, 35g protein, 30g fat, and 750 mg sodium. Air-frying or baking is recommended as a lower-fat, lower-sodium alternative preparation method.

No exact published nutrient analysis for chicken pakora as a single composite dish entry was found in any single approved source. Values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care).

No core macro is zero; all four core macros (energy_kcal 267.0, protein_g 19.4, fat_g 16.7, carbohydrate_g 11.1) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a deep-fried battered chicken product. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour (besan) as the dominant carbohydrate source per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken pakora exists in approved GI sources as of 2026. 4. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for chicken pakora as of 2024. 5. Input ethnic_tags ('Curry', 'Biryani', 'Naan') were removed from the output as they are not relevant to chicken pakora specifically. 6. The previously flagged forbidden source reference has been removed and replaced with direct approved-source citations including specific USDA FDC ID.
